doc.title: Sales with a right of return
doc.event: Sena Co. sells $100,000 of goods (cost $60,000) with a right of return; experience says 10% comes back. Later, $8,000 (sales value) is actually returned. Prepare the sale, cost, and return entries.

variants.label: Book the sale net of expected returns, then true up

lines.label: full amount collected
lines.why: Customers pay full price; the return estimate is the seller's problem.
lines.trap: Variable consideration in the wild: expected returns REDUCE the transaction price from day one — not when the box shows up.

lines.label: the 90% expected to stick
lines.why: Revenue only for consideration the seller expects to keep.
lines.trap: Booking 100,000 and 'fixing it later' overstates this period's revenue — the estimate belongs in the sale entry.

lines.label: expected give-backs
lines.why: A real obligation: cash the seller expects to hand back.
lines.trap: Liability, not contra-revenue-someday — it sits on the balance sheet from the sale date.

entries.caption: Cost side — inventory expected back is still an asset

lines.label: cost of the 90% staying sold
lines.trap: Full-COGS-now overstates expense exactly as full-revenue-now overstates income.

lines.label: cost of the 10% coming home
lines.why: The right to recover returned goods is an ASSET at the old inventory cost.
lines.trap: Two-sided symmetry: refund liability at SALES value (10,000), returns asset at COST (6,000). Mixing the bases is the classic scramble.

lines.label: all of it shipped
lines.trap: Inventory credits for what SHIPPED; the estimate splits the debit side, not this line.

entries.caption: 8,000 (sales value) actually returned — settle against the estimates

lines.label: liability absorbs the refund
lines.why: The give-back was pre-booked — actual returns draw down the liability, not revenue.
lines.trap: Debiting Sales Revenue here would double-count the haircut taken at sale.

lines.label: refund paid
lines.trap: Returns better or worse than estimated? True up the remaining liability through revenue — estimates get revisited, not ignored.

memorize.body: Right of return: Revenue = sales × (1 − expected return %) · Refund Liability = sales × return % · Estimated Inventory Returns (asset) = cost × return %. Sales value on the liability, cost on the asset.
Sales with ALLOWANCES (price concessions) run the same variable-consideration logic — revenue net of expected concessions — without the inventory-asset side.

questions.prompt: 100,000 of goods sell with 10% expected returns. Sales Revenue recorded at the sale date =

distractors.feedback: 100,000 is the CASH. Expected returns are variable consideration — revenue books at the 90,000 expected to stick, with a 10,000 Refund Liability for the rest.

build.scaffold: Two mirrored splits: revenue side splits 90/10 at SALES value; cost side splits 90/10 at COST. Actual returns then settle against the pre-booked liability — never back through revenue.
